title: Bylaws of the Branson School Community Values and Fair Practices Program
subtitle: Governing Document for Institutional Commitment to All Community Members

sections.title: Institutional Mission
sections.technical: Branson School is committed to developing students who make a positive impact in the world by leading lives of integrity, purpose, learning, and joy. This mission is grounded in principles of diversity, equity, and inclusion, recognizing that an inclusive and welcoming learning environment serves all students and strengthens the entire community.
sections.standard: Branson School is committed to developing students who make a positive impact in the world by leading lives of integrity, purpose, learning, and joy. This mission is grounded in principles of varied perspectives, fair access, and welcoming environments, recognizing that a fully participatory learning environment serves all students and strengthens the entire community.
sections.simplePlain: Branson School works to develop students who make a positive impact in the world by living with integrity, purpose, learning, and joy. We believe that when everyone in our community feels welcome and valued, all students do better and our school becomes stronger.

sections.title: Community Values
sections.technical: The Branson School community is founded on four core values: Courage, Kindness, Honor, and Purpose. These values apply equally to all members of the community and guide interactions, decision-making, and institutional practices.
sections.standard: The Branson School community is founded on four core values: Courage, Kindness, Honor, and Purpose. These values apply equally to all members of the community and guide interactions, decision-making, and institutional practices.
sections.simplePlain: Branson School is built on four core values: Courage, Kindness, Honor, and Purpose. These values apply to everyone in our community and should guide how we treat each other and how the school makes decisions.

sections.title: Statement of Community Belonging
sections.technical: Branson believes the well-being of people and the planet are inextricably linked and are essential to creating a truly excellent learning environment and a vibrant, caring community. We aspire to have every member of the Branson community feel a deep sense of belonging and inclusion, with visible representation and voice in all aspects of school life.
sections.standard: Branson believes the well-being of people and the planet are inextricably linked and are essential to creating a truly excellent learning environment and a vibrant, caring community. We aspire to have every member of the Branson community feel a deep sense of community membership and full participation, with visible representation and voice in all aspects of school life.
sections.simplePlain: Branson believes that taking care of people and the planet are connected and necessary for creating an excellent school where everyone feels valued. We want every person in our community to feel like they truly belong, see themselves represented at school, and have a real voice in what happens here.

sections.title: Program Purpose and Scope
sections.technical: This Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Program establishes binding institutional commitments and practices designed to create an environment where all students, families, faculty, and staff feel welcomed, respected, and empowered to fully participate in the Branson community. The program operates across all institutional functions including governance, admissions, curriculum, hiring, procurement, and student conduct.
sections.standard: This Community Values and Fair Practices Program establishes binding institutional commitments and practices designed to create an environment where all students, families, faculty, and staff feel welcomed, respected, and empowered to fully participate in the Branson community. The program operates across all institutional functions including governance, admissions, curriculum, hiring, procurement, and student conduct.
sections.simplePlain: This program sets up rules and practices that help make Branson a place where everyone—students, families, teachers, and staff—feels welcome, respected, and able to participate fully. It affects how the school hires people, makes decisions, teaches classes, buys supplies, and handles student behavior.

sections.title: Key Terms
sections.technical:
For purposes of these bylaws, the following terms are defined as follows:

(a) Diversity: The collective presence and participation of people from different backgrounds, identities, and experiences, including race, ethnicity, gender identity, gender expression, sexual orientation, socioeconomic status, religion, disability, and other dimensions of identity.

(b) Equity: The principle of ensuring fair access, opportunity, and distribution of resources and protections to all individuals, acknowledging and addressing systemic barriers and unequal starting points.

(c) Inclusion: The active practice of creating a welcoming environment where people from all backgrounds feel valued, respected, and able to fully participate.

(d) Belonging: The deep sense that one is welcomed, valued, and has a genuine place within a community.

(e) Marginalized/Historically Excluded Communities: Groups that have historically faced systemic barriers to opportunity, participation, and resources within educational and broader societal contexts.

(f) Microaggression: Subtle, often unintentional communications or behaviors that demean or alienate individuals based on identity characteristics.

(g) Trauma-Informed: Approaches that recognize the widespread impact of trauma and prioritize safety, trustworthiness, transparency, peer support, and collaboration.

(h) Intersectionality: The recognition that individuals hold multiple, overlapping identities that collectively shape their experience within systems and communities.

(i) Implicit Bias: Unconscious attitudes, beliefs, or stereotypes that affect understanding, actions, and decision-making.

(j) Restorative Justice: A practice-based approach to addressing harm that prioritizes accountability, relationship-building, and community healing over punitive measures alone.

(k) Sexual Orientation: An individual's emotional, romantic, and/or sexual attraction to others.
sections.standard:
For purposes of these bylaws, the following terms are defined as follows:

(a) Varied Perspectives and Representation: The collective presence and participation of people from different backgrounds, identities, and experiences, including race, ethnicity, personal identity, self-identification, socioeconomic status, religion, accommodation needs, and other dimensions of identity.

(b) Fairness: The principle of ensuring fair access, opportunity, and distribution of resources and protections to all individuals, acknowledging and addressing organizational barriers and unequal starting points.

(c) Welcoming Environment: The active practice of creating a supportive atmosphere where people from all backgrounds feel valued, respected, and able to fully participate.

(d) Community Membership: The sense that one is welcomed, valued, and has a genuine place within a community.

(e) Historically Excluded Communities: Groups that have historically faced organizational barriers to opportunity, participation, and resources within educational and broader societal contexts.

(f) Subtle Disrespect: Unintentional communications or behaviors that may make individuals feel less valued based on identity characteristics.

(g) Supportive Environment Approach: Methods that recognize the impact of adverse experiences and prioritize safety, trustworthiness, transparency, peer support, and collaboration.

(h) Overlapping Identities: The recognition that individuals hold multiple distinct identities that collectively shape their experience within systems and communities.

(i) Unexamined Assumptions: Unconscious attitudes, beliefs, or generalizations that affect understanding, actions, and decision-making.

(j) Restorative Practices: A practice-based approach to addressing harm that prioritizes accountability, relationship-building, and community healing over punitive measures alone.

(k) Personal Identity and Attraction: An individual's emotional, romantic, and/or physical attraction to others.
sections.simplePlain:
For this document, we use these words in these ways:

(a) Varied Perspectives and Representation: Having people from many different backgrounds and experiences at our school, including people of different races, religions, family situations, and abilities.

(b) Fairness: Making sure everyone has equal chances to participate and access resources, even if they start from different places.

(c) Welcoming Environment: Working to make sure everyone feels valued and able to be themselves at school.

(d) Community Membership: Feeling like you truly belong and are valued at school.

(e) Historically Excluded Communities: Groups of people who have not had equal opportunities or been fully included in schools and society in the past.

(f) Subtle Disrespect: Comments or actions that make someone feel less valued because of who they are, even if it wasn't meant to be hurtful.

(g) Supportive Environment Approach: Ways of helping that recognize if someone has had difficult experiences and focus on making them feel safe and supported.

(h) Overlapping Identities: The fact that every person has many parts to who they are (their race, religion, family, abilities, etc.) that all work together to shape their experience.

(i) Unexamined Assumptions: Beliefs about people that we hold without really thinking about whether they're true.

(j) Restorative Practices: Ways of handling problems that focus on fixing harm and rebuilding relationships rather than just giving punishment.

(k) Personal Identity and Attraction: Who someone loves or is attracted to romantically or physically.

sections.title: Oversight and Leadership
sections.technical: The DEI Program shall be under the joint oversight of the Head of School and the Board of Trustees, with day-to-day administration delegated to a designated Director of Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion or equivalent position. This position shall report directly to the Head of School and shall have authority to coordinate efforts across all divisions and departments.
sections.standard: The Community Values and Fair Practices Program shall be under the joint oversight of the Head of School and the Board of Trustees, with day-to-day administration delegated to a designated Director of Community Values or equivalent position. This position shall report directly to the Head of School and shall have authority to coordinate efforts across all divisions and departments.
sections.simplePlain: A Director of Community Values will lead this program and report to the Head of School. This person will work with the Board of Trustees to oversee the program across the entire school.

sections.title: Board Composition Requirements
sections.technical: The Board of Trustees shall maintain a composition that reflects the diversity of the community it serves. The Board shall track and assess its composition across the following dimensions: race/ethnicity, gender, age, geographic background, professional expertise, parent/non-parent status, alumni/non-alumni status, years of service, and socioeconomic background. The Board shall establish and work toward clear goals for representational diversity in its membership and shall review Board composition annually.
sections.standard: The Board of Trustees shall maintain a composition that reflects the varied backgrounds of the community it serves. The Board shall track and assess its composition across the following dimensions: race/ethnicity, gender, age, geographic background, professional expertise, parent/non-parent status, alumni/non-alumni status, years of service, and socioeconomic background. The Board shall establish and work toward clear goals for representational breadth in its membership and shall review Board composition annually.
sections.simplePlain: The Board of Trustees should include people from many different backgrounds so it looks like the community it represents. The Board will keep track of who is on it by looking at things like race, gender, age, where people are from, their jobs, whether they have kids at school, and their financial situations. The Board will set goals to make sure it has people from different backgrounds and will check on this every year.

sections.title: Reporting and Accountability
sections.technical: The Director of Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion shall prepare an annual report to the Board of Trustees and Head of School detailing program activities, progress toward diversity and inclusion goals, budget allocation, and recommendations for ongoing development. The Board shall establish a standing committee on community values, equity, and inclusive practices to oversee program implementation and institutional accountability.
sections.standard: The Director of Community Values shall prepare an annual report to the Board of Trustees and Head of School detailing program activities, progress toward goals for community composition and belonging, budget allocation, and recommendations for ongoing development. The Board shall establish a standing committee on community values and fair practices to oversee program implementation and institutional accountability.
sections.simplePlain: The Director of Community Values will give the Board and Head of School a report each year about what the program is doing, how we're doing on our goals, how much money we're spending, and what should happen next. The Board will have a committee that meets regularly to check on the program and make sure we're doing what we promised.

sections.title: Budget Authority and Allocation
sections.technical: The school shall allocate adequate and dedicated budget resources to support the DEI Program, including staffing, professional development, curricular materials, community-building initiatives, and data collection and analysis. Budget decisions shall be made with attention to equitable allocation of resources across divisions and programs.
sections.standard: The school shall allocate adequate and dedicated budget resources to support the Community Values and Fair Practices Program, including staffing, professional development, curricular materials, community-building initiatives, and data collection and analysis. Budget decisions shall be made with attention to fair allocation of resources across divisions and programs.
sections.simplePlain: The school will spend money to support this program, including paying for staff, training for teachers, classroom materials, events that bring people together, and research about how we're doing. The school will try to spread these resources fairly across all divisions.

sections.title: Honor Code and Core Expectations
sections.technical: All members of the Branson community—students, families, faculty, and staff—are expected to uphold the Honor Code by sustaining an environment of honesty and integrity, trust, respect, and care. All community members shall treat one another with dignity and shall refrain from any discriminatory, racist, sexist, harassing, bullying, or violent behavior.
sections.standard: All members of the Branson community—students, families, faculty, and staff—are expected to uphold the Honor Code by sustaining an environment of honesty and integrity, trust, respect, and care. All community members shall treat one another with dignity and shall refrain from any unfair treatment, race-based bias, gender-based bias, harassing, bullying, or violent behavior.
sections.simplePlain: Everyone at Branson—students, families, teachers, and staff—is expected to follow the Honor Code. This means being honest, treating people with respect, and being kind. We should never treat someone unfairly because of who they are, make fun of them, bully them, or hurt them.

sections.title: Living Our Values
sections.technical: The community commits to actively maintaining respect and kindness in all interactions. This includes holding one another accountable for behavior that contradicts our core values. Community members shall take responsibility for recognizing and addressing discriminatory, racist, sexist, harassing, bullying, and violent behavior, whether witnessed or personally experienced.
sections.standard: The community commits to actively maintaining respect and kindness in all interactions. This includes holding one another accountable for behavior that contradicts our core values. Community members shall take responsibility for recognizing and addressing unfair treatment, race-based bias, gender-based bias, harassing, bullying, and violent behavior, whether witnessed or personally experienced.
sections.simplePlain: Everyone at Branson should try to be respectful and kind in everything we do. We should call out—in a kind way—when someone isn't being respectful or kind. If we see someone being treated unfairly or bullied, we should do something about it.

sections.title: Civil Discourse Guidelines
sections.technical: The community shall engage in civil discourse—not debate—when discussing topics related to identity, systemic inequity, and sensitive social issues. This approach shall include: (a) acknowledging that certain topics are charged and may have emotional impact; (b) teachers reflecting on their own identity and lived experience before leading such discussions; (c) avoiding the conflation of distinct issues into false "sides" or binary categories; (d) providing support when conversations have emotional impact; and (e) creating a supportive environment where intellectual rigor and emotional safety coexist.
sections.standard: The community shall engage in civil discourse—not debate—when discussing topics related to personal identity, fairness concerns, and sensitive social issues. This approach shall include: (a) acknowledging that certain topics are charged and may have emotional impact; (b) teachers reflecting on their own identity and lived experience before leading such discussions; (c) avoiding the conflation of distinct issues into false "sides" or binary categories; (d) providing support when conversations have emotional impact; and (e) creating a supportive environment where intellectual rigor and emotional safety coexist.
sections.simplePlain: When we talk about difficult topics at school—like fairness, identity, or current events—we should have respectful conversations, not arguments. Teachers should think about their own backgrounds before leading these conversations. We shouldn't act like every issue has only two sides. We should be ready to support people who get emotional during these talks. Everyone should feel both intellectually challenged and emotionally safe.

sections.title: Accountability and Reflection
sections.technical: All community members are expected to engage in ongoing reflection about their own implicit biases, assumptions, and behaviors. The community shall create regular opportunities for professional development, dialogue, and accountability around equitable and inclusive practices. Individuals who harm others through discriminatory behavior shall be expected to take responsibility, seek to understand the impact of their actions, and work toward repair and restoration.
sections.standard: All community members are expected to engage in ongoing reflection about their own unexamined assumptions and behaviors. The community shall create regular opportunities for professional development, dialogue, and accountability around fair and welcoming practices. Individuals who harm others through unfair treatment shall be expected to take responsibility, seek to understand the impact of their actions, and work toward repair and restoration.
sections.simplePlain: Everyone at Branson should think about their own biases and how their actions affect others. The school will offer regular training and conversations to help everyone be more fair and welcoming. If someone treats another person unfairly, they should admit it, understand how it hurt that person, and try to make things right.

sections.title: Non-Discrimination Policy
sections.technical:
Branson School is committed to maintaining an environment free from discrimination based on protected characteristics including race, color, national origin, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, disability, religion, socioeconomic status, or any other identity characteristic. All students and employees have the right to be treated with dignity and respect. Discrimination in any form—whether intentional or unintentional, explicit or implicit—is prohibited.

Specifically, Branson admits students of any race, color, religion, national or ethnic origin, sex, or disability to all the rights, privileges, programs, and activities of the school. It does not discriminate on the basis of race, color, creed, age, gender, gender identity, religion, ethnicity or national origin, sexual orientation, or physical disability in the administration of its educational policies, scholarship programs, or athletic or other school-administered programs. Branson complies with the Americans with Disabilities Act in providing services to students with disabilities.
sections.standard:
Branson School is committed to maintaining an environment free from unfair treatment based on protected characteristics including race, color, national origin, sex, personal identity and attraction, personal identity, self-identification, accommodation needs, religion, socioeconomic status, or any other identity characteristic. All students and employees have the right to be treated with dignity and respect. Unfair treatment in any form—whether intentional or unintentional, explicit or implicit—is prohibited.

Specifically, Branson admits students of any race, color, religion, national or ethnic origin, sex, or disability to all the rights, privileges, programs, and activities of the school. It does not discriminate on the basis of race, color, creed, age, gender, personal identity, religion, ethnicity or national origin, personal identity and attraction, or accommodation needs in the administration of its educational policies, scholarship programs, or athletic or other school-administered programs. Branson complies with laws requiring equal access in providing services to students with disabilities.
sections.simplePlain:
Branson does not allow anyone to be treated unfairly because of their race, national origin, sex, personal identity, religion, financial situation, disability, or any other part of who they are. Everyone deserves to be treated with respect. This applies whether the unfair treatment is on purpose or by accident.

Branson is open to all students regardless of race, religion, national origin, sex, or disability. We offer the same rights, activities, and programs to everyone. We follow the law in making sure students with disabilities can participate fully in school.

sections.title: Identity-Based Harassment
sections.technical: Harassment based on identity—including but not limited to race, religion, national origin, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, and disability—is strictly prohibited. Harassment includes both macroaggressions (explicit, severe acts) and microaggressions (subtle, often unintentional slights, insults, or dismissals). Both forms of harassment create a hostile environment and violate community standards.
sections.standard: Harassment based on identity—including but not limited to race, religion, national origin, sex, personal identity and attraction, personal identity, self-identification, and accommodation needs—is strictly prohibited. Harassment includes both overt and explicit acts and subtle, often unintentional slights, insults, or dismissals. Both forms create a hostile environment and violate community standards.
sections.simplePlain: No one can be harassed or treated badly because of their race, religion, where they're from, sex, personal identity, or disability. This includes both big, obvious acts of disrespect and small, sometimes unintentional comments that make someone feel bad. Both kinds of behavior are not allowed.

sections.title: Transgender and Gender Non-Binary Student Policy
sections.technical:
Branson is committed to providing a sense of belonging, safety, comfort, and healthy development for transgender and gender non-binary students. All policies and practices shall be designed with the student's dignity and autonomy at the center.

(a) Names and Pronouns: Students have the right to be addressed by their chosen name and pronouns without requiring legal documentation or court-ordered name change. School staff shall honor students' requests regarding names and pronouns in all school settings.

(b) Official Records: Students or families may request updates to official school records (including student information systems, transcripts, and documents) to reflect chosen names and/or gender markers consistent with student identity. Such changes shall be made at student request without parental notification if the student is a minor, provided the change does not violate state law. When parental notification is legally required, school staff shall balance the student's safety and autonomy with legal obligations.

(c) Privacy Protections: Staff members shall not disclose a student's transgender or non-binary identity, chosen name, or chosen pronouns to others without explicit permission from the student. Such disclosures may pose significant safety risks and shall be treated with the highest sensitivity.

(d) Restroom and Locker Room Access: Students may use facilities (including restrooms and locker rooms) that are consistent with their gender identity. The school maintains five gender-neutral restroom locations to provide additional privacy options for all students. Locker room accommodations shall be determined on a case-by-case basis in consultation with the student to ensure dignity, comfort, and safety.

(e) Participation in Sex-Segregated Programs: Students shall participate in sex-segregated athletic teams, housing on overnight trips, and similar programs consistent with their gender identity rather than sex assigned at birth.

(f) Overnight Trips and Residential Programs: On overnight trips and residential programs, transgender and gender non-binary students shall be housed with students of their asserted gender identity, as this arrangement best protects their dignity and sense of belonging.
sections.standard:
Branson is committed to providing a sense of community membership, safety, comfort, and healthy development for students with diverse personal identities and self-identification needs. All policies and practices shall be designed with the student's dignity and autonomy at the center.

(a) Names and Self-Identification: Students have the right to be addressed by their chosen name and form of self-identification without requiring legal documentation. School staff shall honor students' requests regarding names and self-identification in all school settings.

(b) Official Records: Students or families may request updates to official school records (including student information systems, transcripts, and documents) to reflect chosen names and/or other self-identification consistent with student identity. Such changes shall be made at student request without parental notification if the student is a minor, provided the change does not violate state law. When parental notification is legally required, school staff shall balance the student's autonomy with legal obligations.

(c) Privacy Protections: Staff members shall not disclose a student's personal identity choices, chosen name, or self-identification to others without explicit permission from the student. Such disclosures may pose significant safety risks and shall be treated with the highest sensitivity.

(d) Restroom and Locker Room Access: Students may use facilities (including restrooms and locker rooms) that are consistent with their personal identity. The school maintains five gender-neutral restroom locations to provide additional privacy options for all students. Locker room accommodations shall be determined on a case-by-case basis in consultation with the student to ensure dignity, comfort, and safety.

(e) Participation in Sex-Segregated Programs: Students shall participate in sex-segregated athletic teams, housing on overnight trips, and similar programs consistent with their personal identity rather than sex assigned at birth.

(f) Overnight Trips and Residential Programs: On overnight trips and residential programs, students with diverse personal identities shall be housed consistent with their asserted personal identity, as this arrangement best protects their dignity and sense of belonging.
sections.simplePlain:
Branson wants transgender and gender non-binary students to feel like they belong and to be safe. Here's what that means:

(a) Names and How You Identify: You can ask to be called by any name you want and use any pronouns you want. You don't need legal paperwork to do this. Teachers and staff should use what you ask them to use.

(b) Official Records: You or your family can ask to change your name or gender marker on school documents and your transcript. The school will do this when you ask. Sometimes we have to tell your parents by law, but we'll try to keep you safe and respected.

(c) Privacy: Teachers and staff will not tell other people about your personal identity or what name/pronouns you use unless you say they can. This information is private.

(d) Bathrooms and Locker Rooms: You can use the bathroom or locker room that matches who you are. The school has five gender-neutral bathrooms so everyone has options. If you need special arrangements for locker rooms, we'll work with you.

(e) Sports Teams and Group Housing: If you do a sport or go on a trip where groups are separated, you'll be in the group that matches your identity.

(f) Overnight Trips: When the school does overnight trips, you'll stay with people of your same identity, not based on sex assigned at birth.

sections.title: Students with Disabilities
sections.technical: Branson School is committed to providing equal educational opportunities for all students with disabilities. The school shall make reasonable accommodations to ensure that students with disabilities have full and equal access to all school programs, services, and activities. Accommodations shall be determined on a case-by-case basis through collaborative process with the student, family, and appropriate school personnel. All requests for accommodations shall be taken seriously and processed in a timely manner.
sections.standard: Branson School is committed to providing equal educational opportunities for all students with accommodation needs. The school shall make reasonable accommodations to ensure that students with disabilities have full and equal access to all school programs, services, and activities. Accommodations shall be determined on a case-by-case basis through collaborative process with the student, family, and appropriate school personnel. All requests for accommodations shall be taken seriously and processed in a timely manner.
sections.simplePlain: Branson wants students with disabilities to have the same educational opportunities as everyone else. If you have a disability, the school will work with you and your family to figure out accommodations you need to fully participate in school. These decisions will be made case-by-case, and requests will be handled quickly and respectfully.

sections.title: Bullying Definition and Prohibition
sections.technical: Bullying is defined as recurring negative, aggressive, or demeaning behaviors directed toward an individual or group, creating an intimidating, hostile, or offensive environment. Bullying may include: (a) physical, verbal, or written aggression; (b) cyberbullying and harassment via social media or electronic communication; (c) exclusion or isolation; (d) spreading rumors or sharing private information without consent; and (e) conduct that targets an individual based on identity characteristics including race, ethnicity, religion, national origin, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, disability, or socioeconomic status. All forms of bullying are strictly prohibited.
sections.standard: Bullying is defined as recurring negative, aggressive, or demeaning behaviors directed toward an individual or group, creating an intimidating, hostile, or offensive environment. Bullying may include: (a) physical, verbal, or written aggression; (b) harassment via social media or electronic communication; (c) exclusion or isolation; (d) spreading rumors or sharing private information without consent; and (e) conduct that targets an individual based on identity characteristics including race, ethnicity, religion, national origin, sex, personal identity and attraction, personal identity, self-identification, accommodation needs, or socioeconomic status. All forms of bullying are strictly prohibited.
sections.simplePlain: Bullying is when someone repeatedly does mean things to another person or group in a way that creates fear or makes someone feel bad. This can include hitting, mean words, leaving someone out on purpose, spreading rumors, sharing private information, or targeting someone because of who they are. Bullying through text, social media, or online is also bullying. All forms of bullying are not allowed.

sections.title: Sexual Harassment Definition and Prohibition
sections.technical: Sexual harassment is strictly prohibited and is defined as unwelcome conduct of a sexual nature that is sufficiently severe, persistent, or pervasive that it creates an intimidating, hostile, or offensive educational environment or interferes with schooling. Sexual harassment includes: (a) unwelcome verbal conduct such as sexual comments, jokes, or propositions; (b) unwelcome nonverbal conduct such as display of sexually suggestive materials, gestures, or images; (c) unwelcome physical conduct including touching, grabbing, or assault of a sexual nature; and (d) conduct by any person including adults and peers. Sexual harassment is prohibited in all school settings, whether in-person or virtual.
sections.standard: Sexual harassment is strictly prohibited and is defined as unwelcome conduct of a sexual nature that is sufficiently severe, persistent, or pervasive that it creates an intimidating, hostile, or offensive educational environment or interferes with schooling. Sexual harassment includes: (a) unwelcome verbal conduct such as sexual comments, jokes, or propositions; (b) unwelcome nonverbal conduct such as display of sexually suggestive materials, gestures, or images; (c) unwelcome physical conduct including touching, grabbing, or physical assault of a sexual nature; and (d) conduct by any person including adults and peers. Sexual harassment is prohibited in all school settings, whether in-person or virtual.
sections.simplePlain: Sexual harassment is not allowed. It's when someone does or says something sexual that makes another person feel uncomfortable, scared, or prevents them from doing their schoolwork. This includes sexual comments, jokes, showing sexual images, unwanted touching, or any sexual assault. It doesn't matter who does it or whether it happens in person or online.

sections.title: Reporting Procedures
sections.technical:
Students who experience or witness bullying or harassment shall report the incident to a trusted adult, including but not limited to: their advisor, teacher, Class Dean, Dean of Student Life, school counselor, or Head of School. Adults who receive a report shall document the report and immediately notify the Class Dean or Dean of Student Life. All reports shall be taken seriously and investigated promptly. Reporters and targets shall be informed of outcomes appropriate to their role.

For adult bullying (bullying directed at students or staff by school staff members), reports shall be directed to the Class Dean, Dean of Student Life, or Head of School.

For student-to-student bullying, reports may be made to the student's advisor, Class Dean, or Dean of Student Life.
sections.standard:
Students who experience or witness bullying or harassment shall report the incident to a trusted adult, including but not limited to: their advisor, teacher, Class Dean, Dean of Student Life, school counselor, or Head of School. Adults who receive a report shall document the report and immediately notify the Class Dean or Dean of Student Life. All reports shall be taken serious and investigated promptly. Reporters and targets shall be informed of outcomes appropriate to their role.

For bullying directed at students or staff by school staff members, reports shall be directed to the Class Dean, Dean of Student Life, or Head of School.

For student-to-student bullying, reports may be made to the student's advisor, Class Dean, or Dean of Student Life.
sections.simplePlain:
If you experience or see bullying or harassment, tell an adult you trust—like your advisor, teacher, Class Dean, Dean of Student Life, counselor, or Head of School. That adult should write it down and tell the Class Dean or Dean of Student Life right away. All reports will be taken seriously and looked into quickly. You and the person who was bullied will be told what happened (in an appropriate way).

If a teacher or staff member is bullying someone, report it to the Class Dean, Dean of Student Life, or Head of School.

If a student is bullying another student, you can report it to your advisor, Class Dean, or Dean of Student Life.

sections.title: Investigation and Remedies
sections.technical: Upon receipt of a bullying or harassment report, the Dean of Student Life or designated investigator shall conduct a prompt and thorough investigation. The investigation shall include interviews with relevant parties, examination of evidence, and documentation of findings. Where bullying or harassment is substantiated, remedies may include but are not limited to: (a) disciplinary action appropriate to the severity of the conduct; (b) counseling or restorative practices for both the student who engaged in bullying and the target; (c) schedule adjustments or class changes; (d) no-contact orders; (e) restitution where appropriate; (f) parent/family involvement; and (g) follow-up monitoring to ensure the behavior has ceased and the target is safe.
sections.standard: Upon receipt of a bullying or harassment report, the Dean of Student Life or designated investigator shall conduct a prompt and thorough investigation. The investigation shall include interviews with relevant parties, examination of evidence, and documentation of findings. Where bullying or harassment is substantiated, remedies may include but are not limited to: (a) disciplinary action appropriate to the severity of the conduct; (b) counseling or restorative practices for both the student who engaged in bullying and the target; (c) schedule adjustments or class changes; (d) no-contact orders; (e) restitution where appropriate; (f) parent/family involvement; and (g) follow-up monitoring to ensure the behavior has ceased and the target is safe.
sections.simplePlain: When the school gets a bullying report, the Dean of Student Life will investigate. They'll talk to everyone involved, look at evidence, and write down what they find. If bullying really happened, consequences can include: getting in trouble (depending on how bad it was), counseling or restorative practices for both people, changing schedules or classes, a no-contact order (where one person can't talk to the other), having to make things right if something was damaged, involving parents, and checking in later to make sure it stops and the person who was bullied is safe.

sections.title: Sexual Abuse and Misconduct Protocol
sections.technical:
A student who experiences sexual harassment, assault, or misconduct may disclose to any school employee. Upon disclosure, the employee shall provide a supportive response, help the student find appropriate support and resources, and immediately report to the School Counselor, Dean of Student Life, and/or Head of School. The disclosure shall be kept confidential except as legally required. The school shall comply with all mandated reporting requirements under California law.

Step 1 - Disclosure: A student may disclose to any employee of their choice and is not obligated to disclose more than they want. The student may have a friend or family member present during disclosure.

Step 2 - Response: The employee receiving the disclosure shall help the student find support and resources, and shall contact the School Counselor, Dean of Student Life, and/or Head of School. The disclosure is kept confidential except as legally required.

Step 3 - Mandated Reporting: All Branson employees are mandated reporters under California law and must report same day. The appropriate agency (child protective services or law enforcement) determines investigation. The student may have an employee or family member present for interviews. Mandated reports are confidential under California law. The student may self-report to law enforcement with Branson employee support.

Step 4 - School Action: The school shall offer counseling support to the student. A disciplinary process shall follow for the accused student according to school policies. The school may delay its investigation if an agency investigation is ongoing. The school shall provide interim measures including schedule adjustments and contact minimization as needed.

LGBTQ+ Inclusive Note: Many discussions of sexual abuse reflect heteronormative concepts. Branson prioritizes the safety and well-being of all students and recognizes that sexual misconduct is not limited to heterosexual interactions.

Reporting Resources:
Local: Community Violence Solutions, SF Women Against Rape, Center for Domestic Peace, Ross Police
National: RAINN, National Sexual Assault Hotline, NSVRC, MaleSurvivor.org

sections.simplePlain:
If you experience sexual harassment, assault, or misconduct, you can tell any teacher or staff member, and they will help.

Step 1: You can tell any adult at school. You don't have to tell your whole story if you don't want to. You can bring a friend or family member with you.

Step 2: That adult will help you find support and tell the counselor, Dean of Student Life, or Head of School. Your information will stay private except when the law says it has to be reported.

Step 3: All teachers and staff are required by California law to report serious cases to child protective services or police that same day. The police or agency will investigate. You can have an adult with you during interviews if you want. You can also report to police yourself with help from a school employee.

Step 4: The school will offer you counseling. If someone hurt you, they'll go through the school's discipline process. If police are investigating, the school might wait. The school will make changes like adjusting your schedule so you don't see them if you need that.

We want to keep all students safe, no matter who they are or who they were with.

You can contact: Community Violence Solutions, SF Women Against Rape, Center for Domestic Peace, or call RAINN (1-800-656-4673).

sections.title: Community Honor Board and Restorative Practices
sections.technical:
The Community Honor Board is a student-led body operating under administrative oversight that uses restorative justice practices to address student conduct violations. The Honor Board shall engage in restorative work at three levels: personal (individual accountability and growth), interpersonal (repairing harm between individuals), and institutional (strengthening community norms and trust).

Restorative practices prioritize dialogue, accountability, relationship-building, and community healing over punitive discipline alone. Where appropriate and when parties consent, restorative conferences, circles, and mediation shall be used to address harm. All processes shall be facilitated in a trauma-informed manner and with appropriate training for student participants.
sections.standard:

The Honor Board shall engage in restorative work at three levels: personal (individual accountability and growth), interpersonal (repairing harm between individuals), and institutional (strengthening community norms and trust).

Restorative practices prioritize dialogue, accountability, relationship-building, and community healing over punitive discipline alone. Where appropriate and when parties consent, restorative conferences, circles, and mediation shall be used to address harm. All processes shall be facilitated in a supportive manner and with appropriate training for student participants.
sections.simplePlain:
The Community Honor Board is made up of student leaders who, with adult guidance, help handle situations when students break school rules. They use restorative practices, which means focusing on understanding what happened, taking responsibility, repairing harm, and rebuilding trust rather than just giving punishment.

The Honor Board works at three levels: helping individual students take responsibility and learn, helping people who hurt each other repair their relationship, and helping the whole community rebuild trust and do better. When possible, the board uses group conversations or mediation to help people talk things through. Everyone involved gets training and support.

sections.title: Boundary Policy for Faculty and Staff
sections.technical:
Faculty and staff members shall maintain appropriate professional boundaries with students at all times. Specifically:

(a) Social Media: Faculty and staff shall not initiate friendships or follow-back relationships on personal social media accounts with current students. Any existing relationships shall be limited to institutional accounts dedicated to educational purposes.

(b) Personal Communication: Faculty and staff shall not engage in private, personal, or romantic communication with students outside of educational context.

(c) Physical Space and Supervision: Faculty and staff shall not be alone with a student in off-campus settings without a clear educational or institutional purpose and without notification to appropriate administrators.

(d) One-on-One Meetings: All one-on-one meetings between faculty/staff and students shall occur in visible spaces (such as offices with windows, open classrooms, or other areas where other adults may observe) to maintain transparency and appropriate boundaries.

(e) Duty to Report: Any faculty or staff member who becomes aware of boundary violations by colleagues shall immediately report the concern to the Dean of Student Life or Head of School.
sections.standard:
Faculty and staff members shall maintain appropriate professional boundaries with students at all times. Specifically:

(d) One-on-One Meetings: All one-on-one meetings between faculty/staff and students shall occur in observable spaces (such as offices with windows, open classrooms, or other areas where other adults may observe) to maintain transparency and appropriate boundaries.

(e) Duty to Report: Any faculty or staff member who becomes aware of boundary violations by colleagues shall immediately report the concern to the Dean of Student Life or Head of School.
sections.simplePlain:
Teachers and staff need to keep professional boundaries with students:

(a) Social Media: Don't add current students as friends on your personal social media or follow them back. School accounts for teaching are okay.

(b) Personal Messages: Don't text, chat, or message students privately for personal reasons.

(c) Being Alone: Don't be alone with a student outside of school without a good reason and without telling an administrator.

(d) Meetings at School: When you meet one-on-one with a student at school, do it in a place where other adults can see you, like an office with a window or an open classroom.

(e) Reporting Problems: If you know a teacher or staff member is breaking these rules, report it to the Dean of Student Life or Head of School right away.

sections.title: Equitable Teaching Practices
Professional Development and Ongoing Learning
sections.technical: The school shall provide ongoing professional development for all faculty and staff that builds capacity for culturally responsive, equitable, and inclusive teaching and community-building. Professional development shall include topics such as: implicit bias recognition and mitigation, trauma-informed practices, LGBTQ-affirming practices, culturally sustaining pedagogy, restorative justice facilitation, and other topics supporting the school's diversity, equity, and inclusion goals. Faculty shall be expected to participate in these learning opportunities and to incorporate learning into their practice.
sections.standard: The school shall provide ongoing professional development for all faculty and staff that builds capacity for culturally responsive, fair, and welcoming teaching and community-building. Professional development shall include topics such as: recognizing and addressing unexamined assumptions, supportive practices for students with adverse experiences, affirming practices for students of all identities, culturally responsive teaching, restorative practices facilitation, and other topics supporting the school's community values and fair practices goals. Faculty shall be expected to participate in these learning opportunities and to incorporate learning into their practice.
sections.simplePlain: The school will offer regular training to teachers and staff on topics like understanding and addressing biases, helping students who have had tough experiences, being welcoming to all kinds of students, using culturally relevant teaching, and restorative practices. Teachers and staff are expected to participate in this training and use what they learn in their work.

sections.title: Hiring and Staffing Diversity
sections.technical: The school shall commit to building a diverse faculty and staff that represents the diversity of the student body and the broader community. Hiring practices shall include: active recruitment of candidates from underrepresented backgrounds; evaluation of candidates against criteria that include commitment to equitable and inclusive practice; and intentional outreach to expand the pool of qualified candidates from diverse backgrounds. The school shall track demographic data on faculty and staff hiring, retention, and advancement to assess progress toward diversity goals and to identify barriers to inclusion.
sections.standard: The school shall commit to building a diverse faculty and staff that represents the varied backgrounds of the student body and the broader community. Hiring practices shall include: active recruitment of candidates from less-represented backgrounds; evaluation of candidates against criteria that include commitment to fair and welcoming practice; and intentional outreach to expand the pool of qualified candidates from varied backgrounds. The school shall track demographic data on faculty and staff hiring, retention, and advancement to assess progress toward representation goals and to identify barriers to full participation.
sections.simplePlain: The school wants to hire teachers and staff from many different backgrounds so the staff looks like the students and the community. The school will actively look for and hire people from different backgrounds, asking during hiring whether candidates care about treating all students fairly. The school will keep track of who gets hired, who stays, and who gets promoted to see if there are problems with fairness.

sections.title: Prevention and Training Framework
sections.technical:
The school shall implement a comprehensive prevention and training framework for sexual abuse and misconduct:

For Students:
- All students in grades 9-12 shall participate in sexual abuse awareness and reporting lessons as part of required Human Development curriculum
- Students shall review policies and work through scenario exercises in advisory groups during fall semester
- Training encompasses all four years of high school

For Employees:
- Background checks and LiveScan fingerprinting required as condition of employment
- Annual review of sexual misconduct policies in training sessions held in first month of school
- New mid-year employees shall receive Boundary Policy training within first 30 days
- Annual mandated reporter training held each fall
- Annual read and acknowledgment of Child Abuse and Neglect Reporting Law (P.C. 11166.5)
- Biennial prevention of sexual harassment and abusive conduct training

For Volunteers:
- Training on policies and procedures before regular contact with students
- All campus visitors must complete VisitU check-in system, which checks ID against national sex offender records and watch-lists

sections.title: Annual Community Programming for Prevention of Sexual Misconduct
sections.technical:
The school organizes comprehensive annual sexual misconduct and sexual harassment prevention and education programs to provide all students with skills and knowledge to keep themselves and others safe, and trains all adults in preventive practices, best practices, and mandated reporter obligations.

Specific Annual Programming Calendar:

(a) Human Development Class (August 2025-May 2026, recurring annually): Required for all sophomore students. Year-two curriculum explores issues related to sexual health and identity, providing accurate current information to foster healthy decision-making. Content includes: consent, boundary setting, developing and fostering healthy relationships, and recognizing unhealthy or abusive warning signs.

(b) Fall Coaches' Institute (September 2, 2025, recurring annually for fall season): Mandatory for all fall coaches and athletic staff. Coach team and administration build community and gain deeper understanding of the school's mission in student life, academics, and community values. Boundary Policy and all student safety protocols from Athletic Manual are reviewed annually.

(c) Boundary Policy Training for Staff (September 3, 2025, recurring annually): Mandatory for all faculty and staff. Head of School reviews Boundary Policy during weekly staff meeting.

(d) Boundary Policy Training for Students (September 8, 2025, recurring annually): Head of School shares Boundary Policy with all students, faculty, and staff in annual training.

(e) Workplace Violence Prevention Training (September 2025, recurring annually): Mandatory for all faculty, staff, and coaches. Annual training completed in accordance with SB 553 and CA Labor Code.

(f) Mandated Reporter Training (September 2025, recurring annually): Mandatory for all faculty, staff, and coaches. Completed annually using professional online training module.

(g) Winter Coaches' Institute (October 27, 2025, recurring annually for winter season): Mandatory for all winter coaches. Same content and requirements as Fall Coaches' Institute.

(h) Spring Coaches' Institute (January 26, 2026, recurring annually for spring season): Mandatory for all spring coaches. Same content and requirements as Fall Coaches' Institute.

(i) Sexual Assault Awareness and Prevention Month Activities (April 2026, recurring annually): School-wide awareness activities for students, faculty, and staff throughout April.

(j) Senior Safety Skills Day (May 22, 2026, recurring annually): Mandatory for all graduating seniors. Professional training includes CPR, assertiveness training, and nationally acclaimed self-defense workshop. Skills covered include: self-defense awareness, conflict de-escalation and resolution, boundary setting, and practical defense techniques.

(k) Harassment Prevention Training (August 2026, recurring biennially): Mandatory for all faculty and staff. School Counselor trains faculty and staff. Biennial schedule ensures all staff receive training every two years.

sections.simplePlain:
The school organizes regular prevention and education programs so all students learn how to keep themselves and their friends safe. Teachers and staff get training on how to recognize problems and what to do if they learn about abuse.

Here is the school's annual prevention calendar:

(a) Human Development Class (August-May, every year): Tenth graders are required to take this class. They learn about sexual health, identity, making healthy choices, understanding consent, setting boundaries, building healthy relationships, and recognizing warning signs of unhealthy or abusive relationships.

(b) Fall Coaches' Institute (September 2, every year): All fall coaches and athletic staff go to training. They learn about the school's values and safety rules for athletics. They review the Boundary Policy and safety protocols every year.

(c) Boundary Policy Training for Staff (September 3, every year): All teachers and staff go to training about professional boundaries with students.

(d) Boundary Policy Training for Students (September 8, every year): The Head of School teaches all students about the Boundary Policy.

(e) Workplace Violence Prevention Training (September, every year): All coaches, teachers, and staff get training about preventing violence at work.

(f) Mandated Reporter Training (September, every year): All coaches, teachers, and staff get training about what to do if they learn a child is being abused or neglected.

(g) Winter Coaches' Institute (October 27, every year): All winter coaches get the same training as fall coaches.

(h) Spring Coaches' Institute (January 26, every year): All spring coaches get the same training as fall coaches.

(i) Prevention Month Activities (April, every year): The whole school does activities to raise awareness about preventing abuse.

(j) Senior Safety Skills Day (May 22, every year): All graduating seniors get training in CPR, self-defense, and how to handle conflict safely. They learn how to recognize danger, set boundaries, and protect themselves.

(k) Harassment Prevention Training (August, every two years): All teachers and staff get training about preventing harassment and treating everyone with respect.

articles.title: Curriculum, Academic Equity, and Community and Belonging

sections.title: Academic Program Values
sections.technical: The Branson School academic program is grounded in three core identities: Community Creator, Curious Contributor, and Inspiring Communicator. These identities guide curriculum design, instruction, and assessment. Academic programs shall be designed to develop in all students: (a) a sense of global and local community awareness and responsibility; (b) intellectual curiosity and agency in learning; and (c) the ability to communicate ideas clearly and persuasively to diverse audiences.
sections.standard: The Branson School academic program is grounded in three core identities: Community Creator, Curious Contributor, and Inspiring Communicator. These identities guide curriculum design, instruction, and assessment. Academic programs shall be designed to develop in all students: (a) a sense of global and local community awareness and responsibility; (b) intellectual curiosity and agency in learning; and (c) the ability to communicate ideas clearly and persuasively to diverse audiences.
sections.simplePlain: Branson's classes help students become three things: Community Creators (who understand and help their community), Curious Contributors (who are eager to learn and contribute), and Inspiring Communicators (who can explain their ideas clearly to different people). All classes should help develop these three things in all students.

sections.title: Culturally Responsive Curriculum
sections.technical: The school shall ensure that curriculum across all disciplines incorporates culturally responsive and culturally sustaining teaching that validates, affirms, and builds upon the identities, knowledge, and ways of knowing that students bring from their home communities and cultures. Curriculum shall include diverse authors, perspectives, and voices. Instructional materials and examples shall represent the contributions and experiences of people from different racial, ethnic, religious, cultural, gender, ability, and socioeconomic backgrounds.
sections.standard: The school shall ensure that curriculum across all disciplines incorporates culturally responsive teaching that validates, affirms, and builds upon the identities, knowledge, and ways of knowing that students bring from their home communities and cultures. Curriculum shall include diverse authors, perspectives, and voices. Instructional materials and examples shall represent the contributions and experiences of people from different racial, ethnic, religious, cultural, gender, disability, and socioeconomic backgrounds.
sections.simplePlain: Classes should teach in ways that respect and build on what students already know from their families and cultures. Books and examples should include voices and ideas from people of different races, religions, cultures, genders, abilities, and family situations. This shows that different perspectives are valued and helps all students see themselves in what they're learning.

sections.title: Classroom Environment Standards
sections.technical: All classrooms shall be organized to create an inclusive and equitable learning environment where: (a) all students feel a sense of belonging and are explicitly welcomed; (b) classroom norms and expectations are transparent and equitable; (c) students are grouped and assigned to work in ways that promote collaboration and reduce ability-based or identity-based tracking; (d) visual representations in the classroom reflect the diversity of identities and experiences; (e) all students have access to challenging, rigorous content; and (f) assessment practices provide multiple opportunities for students to demonstrate learning and competence.
sections.standard: All classrooms shall be organized to create a welcoming and fair learning environment where: (a) all students feel a sense of community membership and are explicitly welcomed; (b) classroom norms and expectations are transparent and fair; (c) students are grouped and assigned to work in ways that promote collaboration and reduce ability-based or identity-based grouping; (d) visual representations in the classroom reflect the varied identities and experiences of students; (e) all students have access to challenging, rigorous content; and (f) assessment practices provide multiple opportunities for students to demonstrate learning and competence.
sections.simplePlain: Every classroom should be welcoming to all students and help them feel they belong. Here's what that looks like: (a) teachers tell every student they're welcome; (b) everyone knows the rules and they're fair; (c) students work together in groups that mix up abilities and backgrounds instead of separating students; (d) the classroom has pictures and materials from different kinds of people; (e) all students get to work on hard, interesting material, not just some students; and (f) there are different ways for students to show what they learned, not just one test.

sections.title: Student Support Infrastructure
sections.technical:
The school shall provide comprehensive student support infrastructure to ensure all students' holistic development and sense of belonging:

(a) Cohort/Mentor/Advisory System: Ninth graders are placed in cohorts of 12-14 students with faculty leader(s) who serve as weekly "home base" and guides. Students in grades 10-12 have faculty or staff advisors. All advisory groups meet weekly in groups of 9-14 for community conversations.

(b) Class Deans: Each class dean supports students' holistic development through academic and co-curricular pursuits, serving as advocates and problem-solvers.

(c) Academic Support: Rand Learning Center provides support for students with diagnosed learning differences or those needing organizational support. Student Teaching Assistants (TAs) by subject provide peer academic support.

(d) Social Emotional Counseling: Headed by a licensed marriage and family therapist (LMFT), the counseling program provides a safe and welcoming environment with comprehensive and holistic approach to student well-being.

(e) Therapy Dog Program: Reggae, the school therapy dog, works with science teacher Heather Duncan to provide comfort and support to students.
sections.standard:
